title: There is no documentation for the allowed values of the ulimit command line option
body:
### Issue Description

In newer versions the `--ulimit` option supports the value `host`, which is mentioned in the documentation, but other values are not documented, and in older versions `host` is not available.

Passing a bad value like `--ulimit=n:131072:1048576` offers this message:

```
requires name=SOFT:HARD, failed to be parsed: invalid ulimit type: n
```

But the question is what the valid ulimit types are.

By trial and error and google I eventually figured out that `nofiles` was the ulimit type I needed, but it would be more helpful if the documentation enumerated these.

I eventually tracked down this list in the Red Hat documentation:

```
core - limits the core file size (KB)
data - max data size (KB)
fsize - maximum filesize (KB)
memlock - max locked-in-memory address space (KB)
nofile - max number of open files
rss - max resident set size (KB)
stack - max stack size (KB)
cpu - max CPU time (MIN)
nproc - max number of processes (see note below)
as - address space limit (KB)
maxlogins - max number of logins for this user
maxsyslogins - max number of logins on the system
priority - the priority to run user process with
locks - max number of file locks the user can hold
sigpending - max number of pending signals
msgqueue - max memory used by POSIX message queues (bytes)
nice - max nice priority allowed to raise to values: [-20, 19]
rtprio - max realtime priority
```

I'm not sure if 100% of these are actually valid in podman, but this could be a good starting point for a reference.

See also: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/61334

### Steps to reproduce the issue

N/A

### Describe the results you received

No documentation of valid `--ulimit` option values

### Describe the results you expected

Documentation of valid `--ulimit` option values

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

Ubuntu 22.04, but it's just a documentation request.

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body: Thanks for your hard work on podman!
This documentation does exist in "latest," google took me to a particular release page.
